does: This paper develops a unified theoretical framework for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with general f-divergence regularization, extending beyond the standard reverse KL approach. The authors propose two algorithms based on distinct sampling principles: one extends classical optimism with a specially designed exploration bonus, while the second exploits the sensitivity of optimal policies to reward perturbations under f-divergence regularization. The work provides theoretical guarantees showing O(log T) regret and O(1/T) sub-optimality bounds that hold across the entire class of f-divergences, offering concrete convergence rates for RLHF training with different regularization choices.
connects: This work builds on the established RLHF framework that has become standard for post-training large language models, but addresses a gap in theoretical understanding. While most practical RLHF implementations use reverse KL regularization (stemming from earlier work on policy optimization and divergence-based constraints), recent empirical studies have explored alternative divergences like forward KL and chi-squared. The paper extends foundational work in online reinforcement learning theory, particularly optimism-based algorithms and exploration-exploitation tradeoffs, adapting these principles to the f-divergence-regularized setting where different divergence choices produce qualitatively different policy behaviors.
enables: By providing a unified theoretical analysis across all f-divergences rather than case-by-case results, this work enables principled selection of regularization objectives for specific RLHF scenarios and guides the design of more efficient algorithms. The sensitivity-based sampling approach opens new directions for understanding how different divergence penalties affect policy learning dynamics. These theoretical insights could support development of adaptive or hybrid regularization schemes that switch divergences based on training phase, and facilitate analysis of RLHF variants in more complex settings like multi-objective alignment or constrained policy learning where alternative divergences may offer computational or empirical advantages over standard KL regularization.
